title: AI System Lifecycle Management
description: A comprehensive module on managing the AI system lifecycle in accordance with ISO 5338, covering AI lifecycle stages, requirements engineering for AI, data management processes, model development and validation, and deployment and operations.

sections.title: AI Lifecycle Stages
sections.content:
ISO 5338 defines a structured lifecycle for AI systems that extends the traditional software and systems engineering lifecycle to address the unique characteristics of AI. Understanding these lifecycle stages is essential for managing AI systems effectively from inception through to retirement.

The AI lifecycle encompasses concept and feasibility, requirements definition, design, data management, model development, verification and validation, deployment, operation and monitoring, and retirement. Each stage involves specific activities, deliverables, and decision points that must be managed to ensure the quality, safety, and trustworthiness of the resulting AI system.

Unlike conventional software development, the AI lifecycle is inherently iterative, particularly in the stages of data management and model development. Models may need to be retrained, recalibrated, or redesigned as new data becomes available or as performance requirements evolve. This iterative nature demands flexible planning and governance arrangements that accommodate change without compromising rigour.

Organisations should tailor the AI lifecycle to their specific context, taking account of the complexity and risk profile of the AI system, the maturity of their development processes, and any applicable regulatory requirements. A well-defined lifecycle provides the framework for coordinated and accountable management of AI system development and operation.

sections.title: Requirements Engineering for AI
sections.content:
Requirements engineering for AI systems involves defining the functional, non-functional, and ethical requirements that the system must satisfy. This process is more complex than for conventional software, as AI systems must address requirements related to data quality, model performance, fairness, transparency, and robustness, in addition to traditional functional and technical requirements.

Functional requirements specify what the AI system is expected to do — the tasks it will perform, the inputs it will accept, and the outputs it will produce. Non-functional requirements address how the system should perform, including accuracy, latency, scalability, and availability. Ethical requirements capture the organisation's expectations regarding fairness, explainability, privacy, and human oversight.

Stakeholder engagement is critical to effective requirements engineering for AI. The needs and expectations of users, affected individuals, regulators, and other stakeholders must be identified and documented. Conflicting requirements should be resolved through structured analysis and negotiation, with clear documentation of the decisions made and the rationale behind them.

Requirements should be expressed in a form that is testable and traceable, enabling the organisation to verify compliance throughout the development and operation of the AI system. As AI systems evolve, requirements may need to be revisited and updated, and the requirements engineering process should accommodate this through regular review and change management procedures.

sections.title: Data Management Processes
sections.content:
Data management is a central concern in the AI system lifecycle, given the critical role that data plays in the performance and behaviour of AI models. ISO 5338 addresses data management as a distinct set of processes that span data acquisition, preparation, labelling, storage, and governance.

Data acquisition involves identifying and sourcing the data needed to develop and operate the AI system. Organisations must ensure that data is obtained in compliance with applicable legal and ethical requirements, that consent is managed appropriately, and that the data is representative of the intended deployment context. Data provenance — the record of where data came from and how it has been processed — should be maintained throughout the lifecycle.

Data preparation encompasses the activities needed to transform raw data into a form suitable for model development, including cleaning, normalisation, augmentation, and feature engineering. These activities can significantly influence model performance and must be conducted with care and documented thoroughly to support reproducibility and auditability.

Data governance establishes the policies, roles, and processes that ensure data is managed consistently, securely, and in accordance with organisational standards. Effective data governance supports data quality, privacy, and compliance, and provides the foundation for trustworthy AI system development. Organisations should assign clear responsibilities for data management and should implement controls to protect data against unauthorised access, modification, and loss.

sections.title: Model Development and Validation
sections.content:
Model development is the process of designing, training, and refining the AI model to meet the defined requirements. This stage involves selecting appropriate algorithms, configuring model architectures, tuning hyperparameters, and iteratively improving performance through experimentation and evaluation.

The choice of algorithm and architecture should be informed by the nature of the problem, the characteristics of the available data, and the performance, interpretability, and resource requirements specified in the system's requirements. Organisations should document the rationale for their modelling decisions and should maintain records of experiments conducted, including the configurations tested and the results obtained.

Validation is the process of confirming that the developed model meets its intended requirements and performs acceptably in conditions that reflect the deployment environment. Validation activities include testing the model against held-out datasets, evaluating performance across different subgroups and edge cases, and assessing the model's behaviour under adversarial conditions.

Organisations should establish clear criteria for model acceptance, aligned with the requirements defined earlier in the lifecycle. Where validation reveals shortcomings, the model should be returned to development for further refinement. Independent review or verification of the model by parties not involved in its development strengthens confidence in the validation outcomes and supports accountability.

sections.title: Deployment and Operations
sections.content:
Deployment involves transitioning the AI system from the development environment into production, where it will operate in real-world conditions. This stage requires careful planning to ensure that the system is integrated with existing infrastructure, that operational processes are in place, and that risks associated with the transition are managed.

Pre-deployment activities include final system testing in a staging environment, preparation of operational documentation, configuration of monitoring and logging systems, and training of operational staff. A deployment plan should define the rollout strategy — whether the system will be deployed incrementally, in parallel with an existing system, or as a full replacement — and should include rollback procedures in case of issues.

Once deployed, the AI system must be monitored continuously to ensure that it continues to perform as expected and that any issues are detected and addressed promptly. Monitoring should cover model performance metrics, data quality indicators, system health, and user feedback. Automated alerts should be configured to notify operational teams of significant deviations from expected behaviour.

Operational management also encompasses change management, incident response, and periodic review. Changes to the model, data, or operating environment should be managed through a controlled process to prevent unintended consequences. Incident response procedures should be defined for handling AI-related failures or adverse outcomes. Regular reviews of operational performance support continual improvement and help to ensure that the system remains aligned with organisational objectives.

title: AI Impact Assessment Awareness
description: An awareness module on the importance and principles of AI impact assessments as addressed by ISO 42005, covering why impact assessments matter, the types of AI impacts, stakeholder identification, the proportionality principle, and alignment with the EU AI Act.

sections.title: Why Impact Assessments Matter
sections.content:
AI impact assessments are a critical tool for understanding and managing the effects that AI systems may have on individuals, communities, and society. As AI is increasingly deployed in areas that affect people's lives — from healthcare and finance to criminal justice and public services — the need for systematic assessment of potential impacts has become widely recognised.

An impact assessment provides a structured process for identifying, analysing, and evaluating the potential positive and negative effects of an AI system before it is deployed or during its operation. By conducting an impact assessment, organisations can anticipate harms, design mitigations, and make informed decisions about whether and how to proceed with deployment.

Impact assessments also serve an accountability function, demonstrating to regulators, stakeholders, and the public that the organisation has taken a responsible and considered approach to AI deployment. In an environment of growing regulatory scrutiny, including the EU AI Act, the ability to evidence a thorough impact assessment is increasingly important for compliance and for maintaining public trust.

ISO 42005 provides a standardised framework for conducting AI impact assessments, promoting consistency, rigour, and transparency. Adoption of this standard supports organisations in meeting their legal and ethical obligations and in building the governance structures needed for responsible AI use.

sections.title: Types of AI Impacts
sections.content:
AI systems can have a wide range of impacts, spanning human rights, social, economic, and environmental dimensions. Understanding the breadth of potential impacts is essential for conducting a comprehensive and meaningful assessment.

Human rights impacts include effects on privacy, freedom of expression, non-discrimination, and the right to an effective remedy. AI systems that process personal data, make decisions about individuals, or influence access to services are particularly likely to raise human rights concerns. Organisations must consider how the AI system may affect the rights of both direct users and individuals who may be indirectly affected.

Social impacts encompass effects on social cohesion, public trust, and community wellbeing. AI systems may influence social dynamics by altering employment patterns, affecting access to information, or reinforcing or challenging existing social norms. Economic impacts include effects on labour markets, competition, and the distribution of economic benefits and costs.

Environmental impacts relate to the resource consumption and carbon emissions associated with the development and operation of AI systems, as well as any indirect environmental effects resulting from the system's use. As awareness of the environmental footprint of AI grows, organisations are expected to consider and mitigate these impacts as part of their broader sustainability commitments.

sections.title: Stakeholder Identification
sections.content:
Identifying the stakeholders who may be affected by an AI system is a fundamental step in the impact assessment process. Stakeholders include any individuals, groups, or organisations that have an interest in or may be affected by the development, deployment, or operation of the AI system.

Direct stakeholders include the users of the AI system, the individuals about whom the system makes decisions or predictions, and the organisation deploying the system. Indirect stakeholders may include communities affected by the system's broader social or economic impacts, regulatory bodies, civil society organisations, and the general public.

Effective stakeholder identification requires looking beyond the immediate context of the AI system to consider its wider effects. This may involve mapping the system's data flows, decision pathways, and downstream consequences to identify all parties who may be affected. Particular attention should be paid to vulnerable or marginalised groups who may be disproportionately affected by the system's outputs.

Once stakeholders have been identified, the organisation should determine how to engage with them throughout the impact assessment process. Meaningful engagement — which goes beyond token consultation — provides valuable insights into the potential impacts of the system and helps to build trust and legitimacy. The methods and extent of engagement should be proportionate to the significance of the potential impacts.

sections.title: The Proportionality Principle
sections.content:
The proportionality principle holds that the depth, scope, and rigour of an AI impact assessment should be proportionate to the potential severity and likelihood of the system's impacts. Not all AI systems require the same level of assessment; a system that poses minimal risk to individuals and society warrants a lighter-touch approach than one that could have significant or irreversible consequences.

Applying the proportionality principle requires an initial screening or triage step, in which the organisation evaluates the nature, context, and potential impacts of the AI system to determine the appropriate level of assessment. Factors to consider include the sensitivity of the data processed, the significance of the decisions informed by the system, the number of individuals affected, and the vulnerability of affected groups.

For high-risk AI systems, a comprehensive impact assessment involving detailed analysis, extensive stakeholder engagement, and independent review may be necessary. For lower-risk systems, a streamlined assessment that focuses on the most relevant impact categories may be sufficient. The proportionality principle ensures that resources are allocated efficiently and that assessment efforts are focused where they are most needed.

Organisations should document the rationale for the level of assessment undertaken, including the factors considered and the conclusions drawn during the screening process. This documentation supports transparency and enables auditors and regulators to understand and evaluate the organisation's approach.

sections.title: EU AI Act Alignment
sections.content:
The EU AI Act introduces a regulatory framework for AI systems that operates on a risk-based classification scheme. Under this legislation, AI systems are categorised as presenting unacceptable, high, limited, or minimal risk, with corresponding obligations for providers and deployers. AI impact assessments play a key role in meeting the requirements of the Act, particularly for high-risk AI systems.

For high-risk AI systems, the EU AI Act requires providers to conduct a conformity assessment and deployers to carry out a fundamental rights impact assessment before putting the system into use. These requirements align closely with the structured impact assessment process described in ISO 42005, providing organisations with a practical means of demonstrating compliance.

ISO 42005 supports EU AI Act alignment by offering a standardised methodology for identifying and evaluating impacts, engaging stakeholders, and documenting the assessment process and its outcomes. Organisations that adopt this standard are well positioned to meet the regulatory expectations of the Act and to demonstrate that they have taken a responsible and evidence-based approach to AI deployment.

It is important to recognise that regulatory requirements may evolve and that organisations should monitor developments in AI legislation and guidance. Maintaining alignment with both international standards and applicable legal requirements ensures that the organisation's approach to impact assessment remains current and robust. Investing in impact assessment capability now positions organisations to respond effectively to the regulatory landscape as it develops.

title: Conducting an AI Impact Assessment
description: A practical module on conducting an AI impact assessment in accordance with ISO 42005, covering planning and scoping, baseline assessment, impact prediction methodology, significance determination, and mitigation measures and reporting.

sections.title: Planning and Scoping
sections.content:
The planning and scoping phase sets the foundation for a successful AI impact assessment. During this phase, the organisation defines the objectives, boundaries, and methodology of the assessment, ensuring that it is appropriately targeted and resourced.

Planning begins with a clear articulation of the purpose of the assessment — whether it is being conducted to inform a deployment decision, to satisfy a regulatory requirement, or to support ongoing governance of an existing AI system. The scope should specify the AI system or systems to be assessed, the types of impacts to be considered, and the stakeholders to be involved.

The assessment team should include individuals with relevant expertise, which may span AI technology, data science, law, ethics, human rights, and domain-specific knowledge. The composition of the team should reflect the nature and complexity of the AI system and the breadth of potential impacts. External expertise or independent review may be warranted for high-risk systems.

A detailed assessment plan should be developed, setting out the activities to be undertaken, the timescales, the data and information requirements, and the stakeholder engagement strategy. The plan should also address how the findings of the assessment will be documented, communicated, and acted upon. Adequate time and resources should be allocated to ensure that the assessment is thorough and credible.

sections.title: Baseline Assessment
sections.content:
The baseline assessment establishes the current state of affairs against which the potential impacts of the AI system will be measured. It provides the reference point for understanding how the introduction or continued operation of the AI system may change the existing environment.

The baseline should describe the relevant aspects of the current environment, including existing processes, decision-making practices, and outcomes for affected individuals and groups. Where the AI system is intended to replace or augment an existing process, the baseline should capture the performance, fairness, and limitations of that process.

Collecting baseline data may involve reviewing existing records and reports, analysing operational data, conducting surveys or interviews with stakeholders, and drawing on published research and statistics. The quality and completeness of the baseline data directly influence the reliability of subsequent impact predictions.

Organisations should document the baseline assessment clearly, including the data sources, methods, and assumptions used. Where data gaps exist, these should be acknowledged and their potential implications for the assessment discussed. A well-constructed baseline enables meaningful comparison and supports the identification of both intended and unintended impacts of the AI system.

sections.title: Impact Prediction Methodology
sections.content:
Impact prediction involves estimating the nature, magnitude, and likelihood of the effects that the AI system may have, drawing on the baseline assessment, technical analysis, and stakeholder input. This is the central analytical step of the impact assessment and requires a systematic and evidence-based approach.

Prediction methods may include modelling and simulation, analysis of analogous systems and case studies, expert judgement, and scenario analysis. The choice of method should be appropriate to the type of impact being assessed and the availability of data. For quantifiable impacts, such as changes in error rates or processing times, quantitative methods may be suitable. For more subjective or context-dependent impacts, such as effects on trust or social cohesion, qualitative methods are often more appropriate.

The prediction process should consider both the intended effects of the AI system and the potential for unintended consequences. Unintended impacts may arise from interactions between the AI system and its operating environment, from the behaviour of users, or from changes in the broader context over time. Scenario analysis and stress testing can help to identify these risks.

All predictions should be documented, including the methods used, the evidence considered, the assumptions made, and the degree of uncertainty associated with each prediction. Transparency in the prediction methodology supports the credibility of the assessment and enables meaningful review and challenge.

sections.title: Significance Determination
sections.content:
Significance determination involves evaluating the predicted impacts to determine which are significant enough to warrant mitigation or further action. This step requires the application of criteria that reflect the organisation's values, the expectations of stakeholders, and any applicable legal or regulatory thresholds.

Criteria for significance may include the severity of the impact, the number of individuals affected, the vulnerability of affected groups, the reversibility of the impact, and the duration over which the impact is expected to persist. The assessment should consider cumulative effects, where multiple individually minor impacts may combine to produce a significant overall effect.

The determination of significance should involve input from relevant stakeholders, particularly those who may be directly affected by the AI system. Different stakeholders may have different perspectives on what constitutes a significant impact, and the organisation should seek to understand and address these perspectives in a fair and transparent manner.

The outcomes of the significance determination should be clearly documented, including the criteria applied, the assessment of each predicted impact against those criteria, and the rationale for the conclusions reached. This documentation forms the basis for decisions about mitigation measures and provides evidence that the organisation has exercised due diligence in its approach to impact assessment.

sections.title: Mitigation Measures and Reporting
sections.content:
Where the impact assessment identifies significant adverse impacts, the organisation should develop and implement mitigation measures designed to prevent, reduce, or offset those impacts. Mitigation is a core purpose of the impact assessment process and demonstrates the organisation's commitment to responsible AI deployment.

Mitigation measures may be technical, such as adjustments to the model, data, or system design; procedural, such as the introduction of human oversight or appeal mechanisms; or governance-related, such as the establishment of monitoring and review processes. The selection of measures should be informed by the nature and significance of the impacts and should be proportionate to the level of risk.

A mitigation plan should specify the measures to be implemented, the responsible parties, the timescales for implementation, and the criteria by which effectiveness will be assessed. Where residual impacts remain after mitigation, these should be documented and communicated to decision-makers and stakeholders, along with an explanation of why further mitigation is not feasible or proportionate.

The findings of the impact assessment, including the predicted impacts, the significance determination, and the mitigation plan, should be compiled into a clear and comprehensive report. The report should be accessible to its intended audience and should be shared with relevant stakeholders, including senior management, regulators, and affected individuals or groups where appropriate. Regular review and update of the assessment ensures that it remains relevant as the AI system and its operating environment evolve.
